title: How the fixer part avoids its own pain by focusing on others
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The fixer's favorite hiding place

I know this part of me well. It's the one that spots someone struggling and immediately starts spinning solutions. The one that can't sit still when someone else is in pain. The one that has a thousand resources, a hundred referrals, and a perfectly organized list of coping strategies ready to deploy at a moment's notice.

Here's the thing. This part looks so good. It looks helpful. It looks caring. It looks like everything our culture tells us we should be. Selfless. Generous. Always there for others.

But I've started to notice something uncomfortable about this part. Something I didn't want to see for a very long time.

It's a hiding place.

A really good one. Maybe the best one I ever built.

Because when I'm focused on fixing someone else's pain, I don't have to feel my own. When I'm handing out resources and strategies and gentle guidance, I don't have to sit with the chaos inside my own chest. When I'm being the helper, the healer, the one who has answers, I don't have to admit how little I actually know about my own suffering.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying helping others is bad. I'm not saying your desire to support people is wrong. What I'm saying is that this part of you, the fixer, the rescuer, the therapist friend, the advice dispenser, might be carrying a lot more pain than it lets you see.

And it's using your kindness as anesthesia.

What the fixer part actually does

Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems therapy, would call this part a "manager." It's a protector part that tries to keep you safe by controlling your environment and relationships. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, would say this part keeps you in a state of hyperarousal, always scanning for someone to rescue, always ready to act.

And I think they're both right.

The fixer part keeps you busy. It keeps you needed. It keeps you valuable. And most , it keeps you from collapsing into the grief, the rage, the terror, the loneliness that lives underneath.

Think about it. When was the last time you just sat with someone in their pain without trying to change it? Without offering a solution? Without suggesting a book or a therapy modality or a breathing technique?

For me, it's rare. Honestly, it's almost painful. Because sitting with someone's pain means sitting with my own. And my own pain is a room I've spent decades learning to avoid.

The fixer part has a very specific job description. It scans for problems. It identifies what's wrong. It gathers information. It creates plans. It takes action. It feels the rush of being helpful, the dopamine hit of being the one who knows things, the validation of being thanked and appreciated.

But here's what it doesn't do. It doesn't feel. Not really. Not deeply. It stays in the cognitive , in the strategy , in the "let me research this and get back to you" . It stays safe.

Wild, right? That something that looks so much like love can actually be a fortress against intimacy. Against true connection. Against the messy, raw, unfixable reality of being human.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses these protector parts and the ways we learn to manage our pain by managing others.

The childhood roots of the fixer

I didn't become a fixer by accident. None of us did.

This part developed for a reason. A very good reason. A survival reason.

Maybe you grew up in a home where a parent was struggling. Maybe they were depressed, addicted, chronically ill, or emotionally unavailable. Maybe you learned early that if you could fix them, if you could make them feel better, if you could solve their problems, then maybe you would be safe. Maybe you would be seen. Maybe you would be loved.

Or maybe you grew up in chaos. Maybe there was fighting, volatility, unpredictability. Maybe being the fixer, the peacemaker, the one who smoothed things over, was the only way to create any stability at all.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma-informed treatment, describes how these parts form as survival strategies. They're not bad. They're not wrong. They're brilliant adaptations that kept you alive in an environment that wasn't safe.

But here's the problem. That environment is over. And this part doesn't know it.

It's still running the old program. Still scanning for problems. Still trying to earn safety through usefulness. Still believing that your worth is tied to what you can do for others.

And meanwhile, the exiled parts of you, the ones carrying the original pain, are still waiting. Still locked in the basement. Still hoping someone will finally notice them.

The fixer part is the warden. And it's a kind warden. A well-meaning warden. A warden who genuinely believes it's protecting you from something unbearable.

But it's still keeping you locked away from yourself.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Because the fixer part desperately needs boundaries, both with others and with its own compulsive drive to help.

The cost of always being the helper

I want to be honest with you about something. This part has cost me a lot.

It's cost me relationships. Because people don't always want to be fixed. Sometimes they just want to be heard. And when you show up as the fixer, you're not really showing up. You're showing up with a toolbelt and a plan, which is very different from showing up with an open heart and empty hands.

It's cost me my own healing. Because I've spent years studying trauma, understanding the nervous system, learning about attachment theory, and using all of that knowledge to avoid actually feeling my own feelings. I became an expert in everyone else's pain so I wouldn't have to be a beginner in my own.

It's cost me my body. Because the fixer part lives in the head. It's always thinking, planning, analyzing. It's rarely in the chest, the belly, the legs. It's rarely grounded. It's rarely present. It's always one step ahead, always preparing for the next crisis, always ready to respond.

And that hypervigilance, that constant state of readiness, is exhausting. It's a slow burn that depletes your nervous system over years and decades.

Gabor Mate talks about this. He says that many of the people who are drawn to helping professions, to caregiving, to healing work, are people who learned early that their own needs didn't matter. That they had to earn love through service. That their value was conditional on what they could provide.

And that's not love. That's a transaction.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work helps us understand how trauma shapes our survival strategies and what it takes to truly heal.

What happens when the fixer stops

I've been experimenting with something lately. It's terrifying. I don't recommend it unless you're ready for some serious discomfort.

I've been trying to stop fixing.

Not completely. I'm not a monster. But I've been trying to notice when the fixer part kicks in. When it starts scanning for problems. When it starts offering solutions before anyone asked. When it starts feeling that familiar pull toward being useful.

And then I try to do something different. I try to just be present. I try to listen without planning my response. I try to sit with someone's pain without trying to change it.

And you know what happens?

I feel my own pain.

It comes up like a wave. Grief for the child I was, who had to be useful to be loved. Anger at the adults who couldn't hold their own stuff. Loneliness from all the years I spent taking care of everyone else and never being taken care of myself.

It's not pleasant. But it's real. And it's mine.

The fixer part hates this. It tells me I'm being selfish. It tells me I'm abandoning people. It tells me that if I'm not helping, I'm worthless. It tells me that people will leave if I'm not useful.

But I'm starting to wonder if that's true. Or if that's just the old story. The one from childhood. The one that kept me safe then but keeps me small now.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did you feel a rush of anxiety? That's the fixer part waking up, scared that you might actually stop doing its job.

It's okay. You don't have to stop all at once. You don't have to fire the fixer. You just have to start listening to it. Start understanding what it's afraid of. Start offering it some compassion instead of just letting it run the show.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It can help you start identifying these parts and the patterns they've created.

Learning to hold both

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I don't have a five-step plan to stop being a fixer. I don't have a magic solution.

What I have is this. The fixer part is not the enemy. It's a protector. It's a part of you that learned a very specific way to survive. It deserves gratitude. It deserves acknowledgment. It deserves to be understood.

But it also needs to learn that you're safe now. That you can feel your own pain without dying. That you can be loved for who you are, not just for what you do.

That's a hard lesson. I'm still learning it. Some days I believe it. Some days I don't.

But here's what I know for sure. The pain you're avoiding by fixing others, it's not going anywhere. It's waiting. It's patient. It will be there when you finally stop running.

And when you're ready to turn around and face it, you might find that it's not as unbearable as you thought. That you're stronger than the fixer part believes. That you can hold your own pain and someone else's at the same time.

That's real connection. That's real healing. That's the thing the fixer part was trying to give you all along, but couldn't, because it was too busy trying to keep you safe.

So maybe the work isn't to kill the fixer. Maybe it's to thank it. To tell it that you see it. That you appreciate it. And that you're ready to take it from here.

One step at a time. One feeling at a time. One moment of presence at a time.

You don't have to fix anything right now. You just have to be here. With yourself. With your pain. With your history.

And that's enough. That's always been enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
